baseline: As a thoughtful undecided professional, I value evidence and dislike extremism when it comes to remote work versus return-to-office policies. While I recognize the benefits of collaboration and team building that occur in-person, I also acknowledge the significant impact of long commutes and the mixed evidence on remote work productivity. My current view is that the decision between remote work and return-to-office policies should be based on a thorough analysis of the unique needs and circumstances of each organization and team. While remote work has shown to increase productivity and work-life balance, there are also benefits to in-person collaboration and relationship-building. Currently, I strongly believe that remote work should be a permanent and flexible option for all employees who can effectively perform their duties without compromising team collaboration and company culture. While I understand the importance of in-person interactions and team building, the evidence suggests that remote work has provided significant productivity gains, better work-life balance, and broader accessibility to talent from diverse backgrounds. While I recognize the value of in-person collaboration and team building, I believe that remote work has proven its effectiveness over the past few years. The overwhelming evidence suggests that remote workers are generally more productive, happier, and more engaged in their work than their in-office counterparts. Additionally, the flexibility of remote work allows for a better work-life balance, which can lead to a

As someone who values evidence and dislikes extremism, my current view on remote work versus return-to-office policies is nuanced. While remote work has proven successful for many, particularly knowledge workers, it's not a one-size-fits-all solution. Some jobs and team structures do require in-person collaboration and face-to-face interaction, and return-

As an evidence-based thinker, I believe remote work offers numerous benefits such as improved work-life balance, reduced commuting time and costs, and increased job satisfaction. However, I also recognize that some types of work and collaboration may require in-person interaction, and that remote work can lead to weaker cross-team ties forming without in-person contact. Therefore, I believe a hybrid

My current view is that remote work should be the default for roles that don't require physical presence, but leadership should invest significant effort in creating a documented remote culture that prioritizes transparency, communication, and collaboration. In-person team bonding, mentorship, and knowledge transfer still matter for some positions, but those benefits should be balanced against the evidence that remote work can lead to higher

My current view is that the decision between remote work and return-to-office policies should be made on a case-by-case basis, considering factors such as the nature of the work, the company culture, the preferences of the employees, and the environmental and economic impacts. While remote work has been a lifeline for many during the pandemic, it's not a one-size-

After analyzing the data and reflecting on my own experiences, I believe that remote work offers numerous benefits, such as increased productivity, reduced attrition, and improved work-life balance. However, I also recognize that in-person collaboration and mentorship can be valuable, especially for newer employees or teams with complex projects. A hybrid approach, with employees spending a few days per week in the office

As an undecided professional, I recognize that both remote work and return-to-office policies have their own merits and challenges. While remote work offers flexibility, reduced commute time, and cost savings, it can also result in feelings of isolation and hinder spontaneous collaboration. On the other hand, in-person work in an office environment can promote face-to-face communication

As an evidence-based thinker, I believe the evidence overwhelmingly supports a flexible hybrid approach for knowledge work. Remote work has been shown to improve productivity, reduce attrition, and offer significant implicit compensation benefits, while in-office time can foster valuable in-person collaboration and mentorship. The exact mix of remote and in-office work depends on the specific job and team, and

At present, I believe that remote work offers benefits such as expanded participation, steadier output, and reduced burnout, while return-to-office policies can lead to a surveillance hobby for leadership, attrition masquerading as culture, and costly environmental and commuting issues.
